summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's announcement of a September 15 'Time Flies' virtual event, arguing it will focus on Apple Watch Series 6 and new iPads rather than iPhones, which are delayed. He explains Apple's decision to hold separate September and October events rather than combining announcements or delaying everything. He predicts a timeline for preorders, reviews, and OS releases, while noting that macOS Big Sur should ship later given its rough beta state. He dismisses skepticism about whether Apple can hold a compelling event without iPhones, noting Apple's events are effective precisely because they don't waste time.
key_points: Apple chose to split announcements into two events rather than delay everything or announce iPhones far ahead of availability
Apple deliberately sets expectations about what won't be announced to ensure the actual products get proper attention
The interconnected release schedule of iOS flavors means iOS 14 must ship before Series 6 watches, even without new iPhones
macOS Big Sur is still rough in beta and should be held until November, separate from the iOS release cycle
Event duration skepticism is misplaced — Apple's events work because they don't pad for length
money_quotes: Apple doesn't like saying in advance what will be announced at an event, but they do like setting accurate expectations for what won't be announced.
For god's sake the event is named 'Time Flies.'
Apple is keenly aware their special events garner outsized attention largely because they seldom waste our time with them.
If they only have an hour of story to tell about the new products, the event will last only an hour.
They want the products that are being announced to get attention, not products that aren't being announced.
It feels natural for virtual events to be shorter than in-person ones — the WWDC keynote was shorter than usual, even though it contained more information than most years.
If I had a say in the matter, I'd hold Big Sur until November.
key_insight: Apple's decision to split its fall events reflects disciplined messaging — each product deserves its own spotlight, and Apple's reputation for compelling events rests on never padding them with filler.
